<!-- ============================================================
INSTRUCTOR CUSTOMISATION — update before rendering for each cohort
Line references are approximate; search for the marker text.

  Line ~175  Step 1 Moodle link (R-Project_Template.zip)
  Line 223: Step 1 Moodle link 
  Line 580/583: A3 Moodle links 
  Line ~928 ff.: A5 Setup Chunk

============================================================ -->
---
title: "Session 1 · Lab 1: R Projects & Quarto"
subtitle: "Project Structure, Data Import, and First Presentation"
author: "Prof. Dr. Jörg Schoder"
date: today
lang: en-US
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    theme: cosmo
    css: ../../css/styles.css
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  message: false
  warning: false
bibliography: ../../lit/my_bib.bib
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)    # data wrangling & ggplot2
library(fontawesome)  # fa() icons
library(openintro)    # ames dataset
library(gt)           # formatted tables
data("ames", package = "openintro", envir = .GlobalEnv)
```

## Learning Objectives and Overview

:::{.callout-note}
## Learning Objectives

- **Set up** a reproducible R project with a clean folder structure
- **Explain** why R Projects and relative paths are the foundation of
  reproducible analysis, and demonstrate this by running code that works
  on any machine
- **Import** data from packages, local files, and URLs
- **Explore** a new dataset with `dim()`, `head()`, and `glimpse()`
- **Create and render** a Quarto presentation with inline R code
:::

## 🧭 How to Navigate This Lab

| Symbol | Box colour | Meaning | When to read |
|--------|-----------|---------|--------------|
| ⚠️ | <span style="color:#e02424">**Red — Action Required**</span> | Run code, answer questions | **Do this in the lab session** |
| 💡 | <span style="color:#2f9e44">**Green — Best Practice / Tip**</span> | Coding tips, shortcuts | Skim in the lab |
| 📚 | <span style="color:#1c7ed6">**Blue, collapsed — Background**</span> | Concepts, context | Read at home |
| ℹ️ | <span style="color:#1c7ed6">**Blue, open — Note**</span> | Short clarifications | Read when you reach them |

**Normal text** and code chunks are the **main content** — work through them in order.

⏱ **30 minutes** for mandatory tasks (Steps 1–5 and the two ⚠️ tasks).
Bonus tasks if time permits.

::: {.callout-note}
## ℹ️ Prerequisites

`r fa('r-project')`, RStudio, and the packages `tidyverse`, `openintro`, `gt`,
and `xfun` are installed.
:::

---

## Icebreaker: Why Does This All Matter?

### Scenario

You are a data scientist at a car dealership group. A former colleague, Anna,
had analysed a dataset of used car listings — `autos_(StockerUIBK)_20240414.csv`
— to identify pricing patterns. The analysis was well received. Six months later
your manager asks you to update it with a new data extract.

You find Anna's folder on the shared drive and open her script. The first line reads:

```r
tbl_autos <- read_csv("C:/Users/Anna/Documents/Uni/autos.csv")
```

**Problem 1 — Path error.** Anna's absolute path does not exist on your machine.
You search the shared drive for the original file. After twenty minutes you find
a file called `autos.csv` in a folder called `final_FINAL_v3`. You are not sure
whether it is the same version Anna used. You cannot tell when it was downloaded.

You load the file anyway and scroll through the script. There are no comments.
Variable names have been renamed mid-script without explanation. Two intermediate
objects — `tbl_clean` and `tbl_model` — appear from nowhere. You eventually find
what looks like the original import buried in a file called `test2.R` on Anna's
desktop backup.

**Problem 2 — No documentation, no structure.** The project was never designed
to be handed over. There is no README, no folder convention, no record of which
script produced which output. Anna's work is effectively lost — not because the
analysis was wrong, but because it cannot be traced or reproduced.

This is not an edge case. It is the default outcome when analysis workflows are
not designed for reproducibility from the start.

**Reproducibility** means that anyone — a colleague, a reviewer, your future self
— can take your code and data and arrive at exactly the same results.
**This matters in science (peer review, replication), in business (audit trails,
handover), and in collaborative work (version control, onboarding).**

The two problems Anna's project suffered from have direct solutions:

| Problem | Solution | Where in this lab |
|---------|----------|------------------|
| Hard-coded paths break on other machines | R Projects + `xfun::from_root()` | Steps 1–2 |
| No structure, no documentation | Folder convention + README files | Steps 1, 3.3 |
| Import not reproducible | `DataImport.R` with date-stamped saves | Step 3 |
| Results not replicable | Quarto — code and text in one document | Steps 4–5 |

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Reproducibility in Practice

Data-driven organisations have invested heavily in reproducible workflows
precisely because the cost of non-reproducibility is high.

Airbnb's data science team publishes openly about their internal tooling.
Their blog post
[*"Scaling Knowledge at Airbnb"*](https://medium.com/airbnb-engineering/scaling-knowledge-at-airbnb-875d73eff091)
describes the transition from ad-hoc analysis scripts to a system where
every analytical result is traceable to code and data —
a prerequisite for knowledge transfer at scale.

The broader reproducibility crisis in science is well documented:
@baker_1500_2016 found that over 70% of researchers had failed to reproduce
another scientist's results, and more than 50% had failed to reproduce their
own. The tools you learn in this lab — R Projects, version-controlled scripts,
Quarto documents — are the practical infrastructure that makes reproducibility
achievable.
:::

### CRISP-DM: A Process That Must Be Documented

The analytics methodology used in this course is **CRISP-DM** (Cross-Industry
Standard Process for Data Mining). It structures the analysis workflow into
six phases:

```{r crisp-context}
#| echo: false

tibble::tribble(
  ~Phase, ~Name,                   ~Content,                                            ~Status,
  "1",    "Business Understanding", "Goal: identify underperforming stores",             "Done (lecture)",
  "2",    "Data Understanding",     "EDA: structure, quality, distributions, patterns",  "Lab 2",
  "3",    "Data Preparation",       "Cleaning, encoding, feature engineering",           "Lab 3",
  "4",    "Modelling",              "Regression model: Sales ~ Price + ...",             "Lab 3 + Demo",
  "5",    "Evaluation",             "R², residuals, OVB check",                         "Demo + Lab 4",
  "6",    "Deployment",             "Performance ranking, managerial memo",              "Lab 4"
) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f5f0fa"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "13px") %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "CRISP-DM: Six Phases of the Analytics Process",
    subtitle = "Lab 1 builds the infrastructure for all six phases"
  )
```

Each phase produces artefacts — cleaned datasets, model outputs, reports —
that must be traceable and reproducible. **Lab 1 builds the infrastructure
that makes this possible:** a clean project structure, reproducible import
scripts, and Quarto documents that regenerate their outputs automatically.

:::{.callout-important}
## The Central Lesson

Without reproducible infrastructure, the CRISP-DM process cannot be
retraced, validated, or handed over.

**R Projects + R Scripts + Quarto = reproducible, portable analysis.**

Everything you set up in this lab will be used in Labs 2, 3, and 4.
:::

---

## ⚠️ Setting Up Your R Project

To build Anna's project correctly — and avoid the problems from the icebreaker —
we follow a defined setup workflow. Here is the complete sequence before we start:

| Step | Action | Result |
|------|--------|--------|
| **1** | Download template, unzip, rename folder | Project folder with correct structure |
| **2** | Create RStudio Project from the folder | `.Rproj` file → portable paths |
| **3** | `R/DataImport.R` — loads/saves to `data/raw/`; `data/raw/README.md` documents sources | `data/raw/` populated and documented |
| **4** | Explore data with `dim()`, `head()`, `glimpse()` | First understanding of data structure |
| **5** | Build a Quarto presentation | Reproducible, auto-updating output |

---

## Step 1 — Project Folder

*Before R can do anything useful, the project needs a home — a folder with a
predictable structure that works the same way on every machine.*

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A1: Create Your Project Folder

1. Download `R-Project_Template.zip`\
   ([Moodle link](https://moodle.ffhs.ch/pluginfile.php/5752746/mod_assign/introattachment/0/R-Project_Template.zip?forcedownload=1))
2. Unzip the archive
3. Rename the folder to `ER114_Session1_XY`\
   **X** = first letter of your last name, **Y** = first letter of your first name\
   *(Example: Maria Müller → `ER014-Session1_MM`)*
4. Open the folder and verify the structure matches the tree below

:::{.callout-warning}
No spaces or special characters in the folder name — this causes path problems
on all operating systems!
:::

``` text
ER114_Session1_XY/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/        ← Raw data (never modify!)
│   └── tidy/       ← Cleaned data (tidy format)
├── R/              ← R scripts
├── qmd/            ← Quarto files
├── figs/           ← Figures (created with R)
├── img/            ← Image files
├── lit/            ← Literature (PDFs, .bib files)
└── README.md       ← Project documentation (root level)
```

✅ **Success criterion:** The folder exists with the subdirectories above.
:::

💡 **Core principle:** Raw data always remains unchanged.
Everything else can be regenerated from raw data at any time.

### Project Documentation with README Files — One Per Folder

Every folder in the project contains a `README.md` — a plain text file that
serves as the orientation document for that folder. The convention is simple:
whenever you add files to a folder, update its README.

| Folder | README contains |
|--------|----------------|
| `/` (project root) | Goals, team, milestones, data sources at a glance |
| `data/raw/` | One row per file: source, date obtained, description |
| `data/tidy/` | One row per file: what was transformed, reference to script |
| `R/` | One row per script: purpose, inputs, outputs |
| `qmd/` | One row per document: purpose, output format |

The root `README.md` is the project's front page — it contains the big picture.
The folder READMEs contain the operational detail. Together they make the project
navigable without opening a single file.

You do not need to write all READMEs today. Build them as you add content,
starting with `data/raw/README.md` in Step 3.

---

## Step 2 — RStudio Project

*The folder now exists. This step links it to RStudio — creating the `.Rproj`
file that makes all paths in the project portable. This is the direct technical
answer to Problem 1 from the icebreaker.*

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A2: Create an RStudio Project

::: panel-tabset
### Task

1. Open RStudio
2. **File → New Project → Existing Directory**
3. Navigate to your folder `ER114_Session1_XY`
4. Click **Create Project**
5. RStudio restarts

### Check

When you click **Create Project**, RStudio loads the new project immediately.
Compare the Screenshot below with your RStudio Window and check if all three indicators of a successful project creation are visible:
```{r screenshot-rproject}
#| echo: false
#| fig-alt: "RStudio screenshot showing project name in title bar, R project logo top right, and .Rproj file in Files pane"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root("img", "Session1", "Screenshot_RProject.png"))
```

1. **Top left** — the window title reads `ER114_Session1_XY — RStudio`
2. **Top right** — the `R` cube icon shows the project name `ER114_Session1_XY`
3. **Bottom right (Files tab)** — `ER114_Session1_XY.Rproj` has appeared automatically

✅ **Success criterion:** All three indicators are visible. If the top right still shows *Project: (None)*, the project was not created correctly — repeat Step 2.


### What Happens in the Background?

RStudio creates an `.Rproj` file in the project folder:

``` text
ER114_Session1_XY/
├── ER114_Session1_XY.Rproj   ← NEW
├── data/
...
```

When you open this file, RStudio sets the working directory to the project root
automatically. Relative paths like `"data/raw/file.csv"` therefore work on
**any computer** — without `setwd()`.

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: What Is a Working Directory?

The **working directory** is the folder R uses as its starting point when
resolving file paths. Think of it as R's "current location" on your file system.

```r
getwd()   # show current working directory
```

Without an R Project, the working directory is wherever R happened to start —
often the home folder or desktop. This is exactly what happened with Anna's
script: `"C:/Users/Anna/Documents/Uni/autos.csv"` only works on Anna's machine.

An `.Rproj` file fixes this: opening the project always sets the working directory
to the project root. The file itself is plain text — it stores RStudio project
settings and should be committed to version control so any collaborator gets the
same setup automatically.
:::
:::
:::

---

## Step 3 — Importing and Saving Data: `R/DataImport.R`

*Recall Problem 2 from the icebreaker: Anna had no structured import routine,
no date-stamped file copies, no documentation of where the data came from.
This step builds exactly that infrastructure.*

There are two fundamentally different ways data enters an R project —
and they call for different handling. All import operations live in a single
dedicated script that you build section by section as you work through this step.

**First: create the script.** In the **Files tab** (bottom right), navigate
to the `R/` folder of your project, then click **New Blank File → R Script**.
The file opens already in the correct location — save it immediately as
`DataImport.R`.

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 Alternative: RStudio Menu Bar

You can also create the file via **File → New File → R Script** in the
RStudio menu bar. This requires an extra step: when saving (e.g. with **Ctrl+S**),
navigate manually to the `R/` subfolder of your current R-Project before confirming. If you skip this,
`DataImport.R` lands in the project root instead of `R/` — not wrong, but
inconsistent with the folder convention from Step 1.
:::

**All import and save operations** go into this dedicated R-script `DataImport.R`. This makes it possible to rerun the complete data acquisition from scratch.
The source and provenance of each dataset are documented in `data/raw/README.md`
— not in the script itself.

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 Installing ≠ Loading

`install.packages()` downloads the package **once** from the internet.\
`library()` activates it for the **current session** — repeat after every restart.\
`install.packages()` does **not** belong in a script — only run it in the console.
:::

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Why Code, Not Clicks? R vs. SPSS

Many students in this course are familiar with **SPSS**, where data import happens
through menus and dialog boxes. This feels intuitive — but leaves no record.
Reproducing the analysis means remembering every click in the right order.

In R, every operation is a line of code in `DataImport.R`. Running it again
on a new machine, with updated data, or six months later — always produces
the same result. This reproducibility is one of the core reasons why R
and Python have replaced SPSS in most professional data science settings.

If you click something, document it as code. If you have to make the same
click twice, write it as a function.
:::

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 Running Code in RStudio

Cursor on a line → **Ctrl+Enter** (Win) / **Cmd+Enter** (Mac) runs that line.\
Select multiple lines → **Ctrl+Enter** runs all selected lines.\
After import, objects appear in the **Environment** panel (top right).
:::

### 3.1 Case 1 — Data Fetched Directly by R

The simplest case: data is available as an R package, via a URL, or through an API.
R loads it directly into memory. Since no local file exists yet, we save a snapshot
to `data/raw/` immediately — with a date in the filename so we always know which
version we worked with.

#### Importing Data from Packages 

Many `r fa('r-project')` packages bundle datasets directly — **no file needed**.
The code below shows what this looks like; we will add it to `DataImport.R`
in the task at the end of this section:

```{r demo-script-case1}
#| code-fold: false
# ============================================================
# DataImport.R — Data Import and Local Storage
# Session 1 · Lab 1
# Document each dataset in data/raw/README.md after running.
# ============================================================

# ----- Section 1: Package / URL / API Data ------------------
## Import from package  ------
# R fetches data 
library(openintro)  # ames dataset
library(ISLR2)     # Carseats dataset
data(ames)                                # housing dataset (demo in this lab)
tbl_carseats <- tibble(Carseats)          # retail dataset — used in Labs 2–4
```

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A2a: Copy and Run the Code, then Check Your Environment Panel

Run the lines above and check whether `tbl_carseats` and `ames` appear
in the Environment panel (top right).

:::{.callout-note}
## ℹ️ Why Does `ames` Show `<Promise>`?

`tbl_carseats` appears under **Data** with its dimensions visible immediately.
`ames` may appear under **Values** as `<Promise>` — greyed out. This is
R's lazy loading: `data(ames)` registers the dataset but loads it into memory
only when first accessed, saving RAM. To force the load, run `ames` or
`glimpse(ames)` in the console.
:::
:::

#### Saving Locally — the Routine

After loading from a package, URL, or API, we always save to `data/raw/` using
the same routine. **Only `my_out_file` changes between datasets:**

```{r demo-save-package}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
## Save imported data to data/raw/ with date-stamped filename. ------
library(tidyverse) # for tidy-Syntax (here magrittr Pipe)
library(glue)      # for building date-stamped filenames

# Adapt the following line for each dataset (only my_out_file changes):
my_out_file  <- glue("carseats_{format(Sys.Date(), '%Y%m%d')}.rds")
# This part stays the same every time:
tbl_carseats %>%
  write_rds(xfun::from_root("data", "raw", my_out_file))
```

Result: e.g. `data/raw/carseats_20240315.rds`.

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Why This Save Routine?

**Why `.rds`?**
`.rds` is R's native binary format. Unlike `.csv`, it preserves all data types
(factors, dates, integers) exactly — no information loss on save/reload.
It is also faster and smaller. The tradeoff: R-specific. For sharing with
non-R users, export `.csv` additionally.

**Why the date in the filename?**
Data changes. Package datasets are updated between versions. API data is
refreshed regularly. The date records *when* you obtained this snapshot —
making the analysis auditable even if the source data changes later.

**Why `format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d")` and not just `Sys.Date()`?**
`Sys.Date()` gives `"2024-03-15"` — with hyphens, which can cause problems
on Windows. `format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d")` gives `"20240315"` — compact,
safe on all operating systems, and still human-readable.
:::

`glue()` makes building the filename readable. The `{}`-syntax evaluates
any R expression and inserts the result directly into the string:

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 The `glue` Package

`glue()` builds strings with embedded R expressions — wrap them in `{}`:

```r
library(glue)
name <- "carseats"
date <- format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d")
glue("{name}_{date}.rds")    # → "carseats_20260315.rds"
```

Alternative without `glue`: `paste0(name, "_", date, ".rds")` — same result,
slightly less readable for longer strings.
:::

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A2b: Save `ames` the Same Way

Add the following lines to **Section 1** of `R/DataImport.R` and run them:

```{r task-save-ames}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false
data(ames)
my_out_file <- glue("ames_{format(Sys.Date(), '%Y%m%d')}.rds")
ames %>% write_rds(xfun::from_root("data", "raw", my_out_file))
```

Verify: does `data/raw/ames_YYYYMMDD.rds` appear?
:::

#### URLs and APIs

The same routine applies when R fetches data from the web:

```{r demo-import-web}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
# URL example: Inside Airbnb Geneva listings
url <- "http://data.insideairbnb.com/switzerland/vd/geneva/2024-09-18/data/listings.csv.gz"
tbl_airbnb <- read_csv(url)

my_out_file <- glue("airbnb_geneva_{format(Sys.Date(), '%Y%m%d')}.rds")
tbl_airbnb %>%
  write_rds(xfun::from_root("data", "raw", my_out_file))
```

:::{.callout-note}
## ℹ️ APIs: Same Pattern

Many institutions provide APIs that allow R to query databases directly —
for example the Swiss Federal Statistical Office (`library(BFS)`).
The import call differs, but the save routine is identical.
Web scraping and APIs will be covered practically in **ER015** and **ER018**.
:::


### 3.2 Case 2 — Local Files

Not all data can be loaded by R directly. **Proprietary data** — internal company
data, licensed datasets, confidential client exports — typically arrives as a file.
@thomasAIValueCreators2025 argues that proprietary data is one of the primary
sources of competitive advantage for data-driven companies precisely because it
cannot be replicated from public sources.

These files must be placed **manually** in `data/raw/` before R can import them.
No save routine is needed — the original file in `data/raw/` already serves as
the local raw data copy.

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A3: Download, Place, and Import Local Files

**Step 1 — In your browser:** download these two files:

1. `autos_(StockerUIBK)_20240414.csv`\
   ([Moodle link](https://moodle.ffhs.ch/pluginfile.php/5752753/mod_assign/introattachment/0/autos_%28StockerUIBK%29_20240414.csv?forcedownload=1))

2. `buli_raw_2023-09-06.rds`\
   ([Moodle link](https://moodle.ffhs.ch/pluginfile.php/5752753/mod_assign/introattachment/0/buli_raw_2023-09-06.rds?forcedownload=1))

**Step 2 — In Finder / Explorer:** move both files unchanged into `data/raw/`.
Always keep the original filename — do not rename files.

Verify the folder contents:
``` text
data/raw/
├── autos_(StockerUIBK)_20240414.csv
└── buli_raw_2023-09-06.rds
```

**Step 3 — Back in RStudio:** add Section 2 to `R/DataImport.R` and run it:

```{r task-import-local}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false
# ----- Section 2: Local Files -------------------------------
# Files placed manually in data/raw/ — import with original filename.
# Only my_in_file changes between datasets.

my_in_file <- "autos_(StockerUIBK)_20240414.csv"
tbl_autos  <- read_csv2(xfun::from_root("data", "raw", my_in_file))

my_in_file <- "buli_raw_2023-09-06.rds"
tbl_buli   <- read_rds(xfun::from_root("data", "raw", my_in_file))
```

Verify: do `tbl_autos` and `tbl_buli` appear in the Environment panel?
:::

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 `read_csv` vs. `read_csv2`

`,` as separator → `read_csv()` · `;` as separator → `read_csv2()`\
If in doubt: open the file in a text editor and check.\
Wrong separator → all values land in **one** column!

The hyphens in `buli_raw_2023-09-06.rds` are fine *in this filename* — the
producer chose that format. The issue with hyphens arises only when *we*
generate filenames with `Sys.Date()`, which is why we use `format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d")`.
:::

**What if the file has no date in its name?**
Not every external file carries version information — a client might send `data.csv`.
In that case the download date goes into `data/raw/README.md`. The file itself
is kept exactly as received — never renamed.

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 Why Only `my_in_file` Changes

Keeping the routine stable — and changing only the filename variable —
means adapting the import for a new dataset is always just one line.
The code is easier to read, easier to audit, and harder to get wrong.

`xfun::from_root("data", "raw", my_in_file)` builds the path from the
project root, regardless of which subfolder the script runs from.
This is the same function that makes all paths in this project portable
(see Step 2 — Why does this matter?).

Note that the same principle applies to `my_out_file` in Case 1
:::

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Paths and `xfun::from_root()`

```r
# ❌ Absolute path — only works on THIS machine
read_csv("C:/Users/Maria/Desktop/carseats.csv")

# ❌ Relative path without a project — depends on working directory
read_csv("data/raw/carseats.csv")

# ✅ from_root() — always relative to the .Rproj file
read_rds(xfun::from_root("data", "raw", "carseats_20240315.rds"))
```

`xfun::from_root()` searches for the `.Rproj` file and builds the path from
there, joining arguments with the correct separator for the operating system.
:::

### 3.3 Document in `data/raw/README.md`

The README convention was introduced in Step 1. Now fill in `data/raw/README.md`
with one row per dataset. This is where version information lives for files that
carry no date in their own name:

``` markdown
# data/raw — Raw Data

All files here are original, unmodified data. **Never edit these files.**
Transformations go in R/DataWrangling.R; cleaned data goes to data/tidy/.

| File | Source | Obtained | Description |
|------|--------|----------|-------------|
| carseats_20240315.rds | ISLR2 R package (v1.0) | 2024-03-15 | Retail: 400 stores, 11 vars |
| ames_20240315.rds | openintro R package | 2024-03-15 | Housing: 2930 obs, 82 vars |
| autos_(StockerUIBK)_20240414.csv | Provided by course instructor, downloaded via Moodle | 2024-04-14 | Car listings |
| buli_raw_2023-09-06.rds | Provided by course instructor, downloaded via Moodle | 2023-09-06 | Bundesliga match data |
```

Update this file whenever a new dataset is added to `data/raw/`.

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Data Wrangling and `data/tidy/`

When raw data needs cleaning or transformation — renaming columns, fixing data
types, filtering rows — this goes into `R/DataWrangling.R`. The result is saved
to `data/tidy/` with the same date-stamped routine:

```r
tbl_tidy <- tbl_carseats %>%
  mutate(ShelveLoc = factor(ShelveLoc, levels = c("Bad", "Medium", "Good")))

my_out_file <- glue("carseats_tidy_{format(Sys.Date(), '%Y%m%d')}.rds")
tbl_tidy %>% write_rds(xfun::from_root("data", "tidy", my_out_file))
```

`data/tidy/README.md` documents what was changed and references the script.
Data wrangling begins in **Lab 3**.
:::

---

## 🔵 Demo — A First Look at the Data

*Proper Exploratory Data Analysis — distributions, relationships, outliers —
comes in Lab 2 (EDA). Here we just verify that the imported datasets look
reasonable and get a feel for their structure.*

:::: panel-tabset
### dim() / nrow() / ncol()

```{r demo-dim}
dim(ames)    # Result: [rows, columns]
nrow(ames)   # rows only
ncol(ames)   # columns only
```

❓ What do these numbers mean in context?\
→ *2930 rows = 2930 houses sold; 82 columns = 82 variables (price, area, location …)*

### head()

```{r demo-head}
head(ames)   # first 6 rows (default)
```

❓ `head(ames, n = 10)` — what changes?

### glimpse()

```{r demo-glimpse}
glimpse(ames)
```

:::{.callout-note}
## ℹ️ `head()` vs. `glimpse()`

`head()` shows the first rows as a table — practical with few columns.\
`glimpse()` flips the perspective: each **variable** becomes a row,
including its data type (`<fct>`, `<int>`, `<dbl>`). Ideal for many
columns or a quick check of data types.
:::
::::

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A4: Explore `tbl_autos` and `tbl_buli`

Apply the same commands to the two local datasets you imported in A3.

```{r task-dim}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false
dim(tbl_autos);  nrow(tbl_autos);  ncol(tbl_autos)
dim(tbl_buli);   nrow(tbl_buli);   ncol(tbl_buli)
```

❓ What does one row represent in each dataset?
*(One car listing? One match? One matchday?)*

```{r task-glimpse}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false
glimpse(tbl_autos)
glimpse(tbl_buli)
```

❓ What variable types appear? (`<chr>`, `<dbl>`, `<date>`, `<fct>` …)

Note your observations — they will be useful for the Quarto slides in A4.
:::

---

## 🔵 Demo — Understanding Quarto

### Why Quarto?

Quarto combines **`r fa('r-project')` code, results, and text** in a single
document. Tables and figures are generated directly during rendering —
automatically, reproducibly, and always up to date.

| Format | Use case |
|--------|----------|
| HTML | Interactive reports, websites |
| PDF | Submissions, publications |
| Word (`.docx`) | Collaborative editing |
| Presentation (Reveal.js) | Talks, these labs |

### The Three Building Blocks

A `.qmd` file always consists of three elements:

**1. YAML header** (metadata & output format)

```
---
title: "My Report"
format: html
---
```

**2. Code chunks** — insert with **Ctrl+Alt+I**:

```{r demo-echo}
#| echo: true
4 + 3   # echo: true → shows code and result (default)
```

```{r demo-echofalse}
#| echo: false
4 + 3   # echo: false → result only, code hidden
```

```{r demo-evalfalse}
#| eval: false
4 + 3   # eval: false → code shown, not executed
```

**3. Text** — Quarto uses Markdown: `**bold**`, `*italic*`, `# Heading`.

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: What Is Markdown?

Markdown is a lightweight markup language: plain text with simple
formatting conventions that render as formatted HTML.

| Markdown | Renders as |
|---------|-----------|
| `**bold**` | **bold** |
| `*italic*` | *italic* |
| `# Heading 1` | Large heading |
| `- item` | Bullet list |
| `` `code` `` | `inline code` |

A complete reference: [Quarto Markdown Basics](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html).
:::

### Tables in Quarto Documents

The `gt` package produces publication-quality tables:

```{r demo-gt}
ames %>%
  select(area, price, Neighborhood, Bedroom.AbvGr, Lot.Area) %>%
  head(5) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Ames Housing — first 5 rows (selection)",
    subtitle = "Source: openintro package"
  )
```

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: The Pipe `%>%`

The pipe operator `%>%` chains operations: `x %>% f()` means "take `x`
and pass it as the first argument to `f()`". It was introduced by the
`magrittr` package (2013) and is available via `library(tidyverse)`.

Base R added a native pipe `|>` in version 4.1.0, which works identically
for most uses. We use `%>%` throughout this course because it offers
additional functionality (e.g. with `lm()`).
:::

---

## 🔵 Demo — Inline `r fa('r-project')` Code {#inline}

### The Idea

Code chunks produce **blocks** — tables, plots, printed output.
But sometimes you need a single computed value *inside a sentence*.
That is what **inline R code** is for.

**Syntax:** a backtick, the letter `r`, a space, an R expression, a closing backtick.

<div style="background:#f1f3f5; border-left:4px solid #502479; padding:10px 16px; font-family:monospace; font-size:0.95em; margin:1em 0;">
The Ames dataset contains &#96;r nrow(ames)&#96; houses sold,
with a mean sale price of &#96;r scales::dollar(round(mean(ames$price), 0))&#96;.
</div>

When Quarto renders this, it evaluates the expressions and inserts the results:

> The Ames dataset contains **`r nrow(ames)`** houses sold,
> with a mean sale price of **`r scales::dollar(round(mean(ames$price), 0))`**.

### Why This Matters

Compare the two approaches side by side:

| Approach | Source text | Risk |
|----------|------------|------|
| ❌ Hard-coded | `...sold, with a mean price of $170,000.` | Wrong the moment data changes |
| ✅ Inline code | `...sold, with a mean price of` `` `r scales::dollar(round(mean(ames$price),0))` `` | Always correct, recomputed at every render |

Hard-coded numbers create silent errors: the text becomes wrong without any
warning. With inline code the document cannot be out of sync with the data.

### More Examples

| What you write | What you get |
|---------------|-------------|
| `` `r nrow(ames)` `` | `r nrow(ames)` |
| `` `r ncol(ames)` `` | `r ncol(ames)` |
| `` `r round(mean(ames$price), 0)` `` | `r round(mean(ames$price), 0)` |
| `` `r Sys.Date()` `` | `r Sys.Date()` |

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 When Inline Code, When a Chunk?

Inline code → **single values** embedded in text (a count, a mean, today's date).\
Code chunk → **tables, plots, or multi-line output**.
:::

With data imported (A2/A3), explored (A4), and documented we now learn the
tools that make analysis and reporting reproducible.


---

## ⚠️ Create a Quarto Presentation

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A5: Build `DataOverview.qmd`

::::::: panel-tabset
### 1. Create File

1. **File → New File → Quarto Presentation → Reveal.js**
2. Save as `qmd/DataOverview.qmd`
3. Delete all example content
4. Insert YAML header:

``` yaml
---
title: "Data Overview"
subtitle: "Session 1 · Lab 1"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---
```

### 2. Setup Chunk

Directly after the YAML — loads all objects invisibly:

```` markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
library(ISLR2)
library(gt)
tbl_carseats <- tibble(Carseats)

my_in_file <- "buli_raw_2023-09-06.rds"
tbl_buli   <- read_rds(xfun::from_root("data", "raw", my_in_file))
```
````

:::{.callout-note}
## ℹ️ `#| include: false`

Code runs during rendering but does **not** appear in the output.\
Both objects are available on every subsequent slide.
:::

### 3. Slide: Carseats

```` markdown
## Carseats Dataset

The dataset contains **`nrow(tbl_carseats)`** observations
and **`ncol(tbl_carseats)`** variables.

```{{r}}
tbl_carseats %>%
  head(5) %>%
  select(1:6) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "First 5 rows: Carseats (selection)")
```
````

### 4. Slide: Bundesliga

```` markdown
## Bundesliga Dataset

The dataset contains **`nrow(tbl_buli)`** observations
and **`ncol(tbl_buli)`** variables.

```{{r}}
tbl_buli %>%
  head(5) %>%
  select(1:6) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "First 5 rows: Bundesliga (selection)")
```
````

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 `select(1:6)`

Selects the first 6 columns — prevents overfull slides with wide datasets.
:::

### 5. Slide: Comparison

``` markdown
## Dataset Overview

| Dataset    | Rows                   | Columns                |
|------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Carseats   | `nrow(tbl_carseats)`   | `ncol(tbl_carseats)`   |
| Bundesliga | `nrow(tbl_buli)`       | `ncol(tbl_buli)`       |
```

### 6. Render & Check

1. **Ctrl+S** — save
2. **Render** (or **Ctrl+Shift+K**)
3. Presentation opens in the browser

✅ **Success criterion:** Both slides show correct dimensions and tables.

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 Render Error?

Read the line number in the error message, isolate the affected chunk
in the console and test it there first.
:::
:::::::
:::

---

## Bonus Tasks

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B1: Variable Types as a Table

Add a slide showing variable names and data types for `tbl_carseats`:

```{r bonus-b1}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
tibble(
  Variable = names(tbl_carseats),
  Type     = map_chr(tbl_carseats, class)
) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "Variables: Carseats")
```
:::

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B2: Missing Values

Show only columns with at least one `NA` in `tbl_carseats`:

```{r bonus-b2}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
tbl_carseats %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to  = "Variable",
               values_to = "Missing") %>%
  filter(Missing > 0) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "Missing values: Carseats")
```
:::

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B3: Visualisation with `ames`

```{r bonus-b3}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
ames %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = area, y = price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "#502479") +         # --- colour ---
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(
    title    = "Living Area vs. Sale Price",
    subtitle = "Ames, Iowa 2006–2010",
    x        = "Living area (sqft)",
    y        = "Sale price (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B4: Change the Presentation Theme

Experiment with the `theme` parameter in the YAML of `DataOverview.qmd`:

``` yaml
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: moon
```

Options: `sky` · `beige` · `serif` · `simple` · `night` · `moon`

❓ Which theme is appropriate for an academic presentation?
:::

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B5: Load Airbnb Data from the Web

```{r bonus-b5}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
# Read current Geneva Airbnb listings directly from Inside Airbnb
url_airbnb <- "http://data.insideairbnb.com/switzerland/vd/geneva/2024-09-18/data/listings.csv.gz"
tbl_airbnb <- read_csv(url_airbnb)

dim(tbl_airbnb)
glimpse(tbl_airbnb)
```

How many listings are there? What variables are available?
Could this data answer a business question?
*(Hint: think about price, neighbourhood, room type.)*
:::

---

## Common Errors & Solutions

:::{.callout-warning}
#### ❌ File Not Found

```
Error: cannot open file 'data/raw/...'
```

**Cause:** No `r fa('r-project')` project open, or a typo in the path.

**Solution:**\
1. Is the project name visible in the top right of RStudio?\
2. Is the file really in `data/raw/`?\
3. Use `xfun::from_root()`
:::

:::{.callout-warning}
#### ❌ All Data in One Column

Dataset has only one column, although several are expected.

**Cause:** Wrong separator — `read_csv()` instead of `read_csv2()` (or vice versa).

**Solution:** Open the file in a text editor and check: comma or semicolon?
:::

:::{.callout-warning}
#### ❌ Package Not Installed

```
Error: there is no package called 'gt'
```

**Solution:** Install once, then load:

``` r
install.packages("gt")
library(gt)
```

Never write `install.packages()` in a script — only run it in the console!
:::

:::{.callout-warning}
#### ❌ Object Not Found

```
Error: object 'tbl_carseats' not found
```

**Cause:** Import code was not executed.

**Solution:**\
- In the script: run import lines first\
- In Quarto: check that the setup chunk with `#| include: false` is present
:::

---

## Lab 1 · Summary

:::{.callout-tip}
## What We Have Learned

✅ R Projects guarantee reproducible, machine-independent paths\
✅ **Import–save workflow:** fetch or load → `write_rds()` with date in filename (Case 1) or place file manually (Case 2) → document in `data/raw/README.md`\
✅ `format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d")` — safe, human-readable date format for filenames\
✅ `glue()` — readable string building with embedded R expressions\
✅ `xfun::from_root("data", "raw", filename)` — paths always relative to `.Rproj`\
✅ Local files keep their original names; package/API data gets a new date-stamped name\
✅ `dim()`, `head()`, `glimpse()` for a first overview of any dataset\
✅ Inline R code keeps documents automatically consistent
:::

:::{.callout-important}
## Checklist Before Submission

☐ Folder named correctly (`ER114_Session1_XY`)\
☐ `.Rproj` file present and open\
☐ Both files in `data/raw/` (unchanged)\
☐ `R/DataImport.R` runs **without errors** from top to bottom\
☐ `data/raw/` contains `carseats_YYYYMMDD.rds` and `ames_YYYYMMDD.rds`\
☐ `data/raw/README.md` documents all four datasets\
☐ `qmd/DataOverview.qmd` (Task A5) renders without errors\
☐ Both datasets shown in the presentation with correct dimensions\
☐ Comparison slide: inline code shows correct values
:::

:::{.callout-note}
## ℹ️ What Comes Next

In **Lab 2**, we apply the infrastructure built here to the Carseats dataset
from the `ISLR2` package — a retail dataset of 400 stores that will be the
running example throughout the course. The import is a one-liner:

```r
library(ISLR2)
tbl_carseats <- tibble(Carseats)
```

The datasets imported today (`ames`, `tbl_carseats`, `tbl_autos`, `tbl_buli`) served as practice vehicles for learning the import mechanics and to prepare you for the revision assignment and upcoming labs. In Lab 2 the analytical work begins.

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Outlook: R Projects + Git = Full Reproducibility

R Projects solve the path problem. **Git** solves the history problem:
it tracks every change to every file, allows you to revert to any previous
state, and enables collaboration without overwriting each other's work.

R Project + Git repository = the gold standard for reproducible analysis.
Git will be introduced in **ER015**. For now: treat your `.Rproj` file as
the anchor of your project — keep it at the root, never move it.
:::
:::

---

## Resources

📖 [r4ds.hadley.nz](https://r4ds.hadley.nz) — Ch. 6 & 7\
*(Workflow: scripts & projects)*

📖 [quarto.org/docs/output-formats](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-basics.html)\
*(HTML documents in Quarto)*

📖 [xfun documentation](https://yihui.org/xfun/)\
*(from_root())*

📖 [insideairbnb.com/get-the-data](http://insideairbnb.com/get-the-data/)\
*(Open Airbnb listing data for 100+ cities)*

📺 RStudio: *"Project-oriented workflow"* (Webinar)

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Self-Learning: Reference Management with Zotero

Zotero is a free reference manager that integrates with RStudio and Quarto.
It is relevant from **Lab 3 onwards** when citations appear in reports.

Setup steps:
1. Download from [zotero.org](https://www.zotero.org) and create a free account
2. Install **Better BibTeX** add-on ([instructions](https://retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/installation/))
3. Install the [Zotero Connector](http://www.zotero.org/download/connectors) for your browser
4. Export library: `File > Export Library` → format: CSL JSON → save as `lit/my_bib.json`
5. Add to YAML: `bibliography: ../../lit/my_bib.json`

Note: Zotero also integrates directly with MS Word.
Always save `.R` and `.qmd` files with **UTF-8 encoding** to avoid compatibility issues.
:::

<br> **Questions? → Lab 1 Debrief**

---

## References
